content.full_text:
UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, LOS ANGELES
UCLA
SANTA BARBANA . SANTA CRUZ
SCHOOL OF LAW
405 HILGARD AVENUE
LOS ANCELES, CALIFORNIA 90024- 147 6
September 26, I99O
Jeanne Pritchard
Fai-r Political Practices Commission
Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807
Dear Ms. Pritchard,
This is a reguest for advice submitted in behalf of Voter
Guide, a publisher of slate mail.
In a letter dated August 17, 1988, a copy of which is
enclosed, you advised Michael Berman in behalf of Voter Guide
that for the l-988 election, it should provide copies of its
expenditure schedules to each of its slate mail customers, for
them to attach to their own campaign statements.
Since the receipt of this letter, Voter Guide has been fol-
Iowing the practice you recommended, and it will continue to do
so if that is what is reguired. 
Although not overwhelrning, the
burden of providing copies of the expenditure schedules to a long
list of customers under the tight deadlines for filing 
campaign
reports is significant. 
The public benefit seems mj-nimal, since
Voter Guide will appear on the expenditure schedule of each
customer, and any interested person can easily examine a copy of
Voter Guide's reports. 
AIso, it is my understanding that few if
any slate rnail organizations besides Voter Guide are complying
with the advice contained in the 1988 letter.
Your letter in 1-988 indicateci that, the advice rnj-ght be
temporary in nature, and subject to reconsideration. 
Thus, I
request advice regarding the staff's current view of whether a
slate mail organization that files reports in accordance with the
requirements of the Act has any additional duties by way of
providing information to its customers, and if so what Lhose
duties are.
This question wirr have littre 
or no practicar- effect on
Voter Guide untir the final pre-election report, which is due
october 25. 
So that they can plan their activities with knowl-
BERKELEY . DAVIS . INVINE . LOS ANGELES ' RIVERSIDE . SAN DIEGO .


-2-
edge of what wiII be required, I would appreciate a response at
l-east a r^reek .in advance of that date. 
I hope this gives you
sufficient time. 
Recognizing the Lrorkload imposed on the staff
by reason of the confusion resulting from current judicial pro-
ceedings, I shal1 understand if the response consists of
teLephone advice with written confinnation later.
I subnit this as a request for infornal assistance under
Regulation 18329(c), but if it viII be more expeditious or in any
way preferabl-e to treat it under a different category, you may
deem it as so submitted. 
Please let me know if you require any
additional i-nforrnation.
Daniel H. Lowenstein
Attorney for Voter cuide
cc: Mary EIlen Padil-la

sections.question: Does a slate mail organization that files reports in accordance with the requirements of the Act have any additional duties by way of providing information to its customers, and if so what those duties are?
sections.question_synthetic: A slate mail publisher (Voter Guide) seeks clarification on whether it must provide copies of its expenditure schedules to customers for attachment to their campaign statements, following up on prior FPPC advice from 1988 that the organization has been implementing but finds burdensome.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or FPPC advice is present in this document. The letter references prior FPPC advice from August 17, 1988 but that prior letter is not included in this document. Requestor seeks expedited response before October 25, 1990 deadline.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Does a slate mail organization that files reports in accordance with the requirements of the Act have any additional duties by way of providing information to its customers, and if so what those duties are?

embedding.summary: UCLA Law School attorney Daniel Lowenstein requests advice on behalf of Voter Guide, a slate mail publisher, regarding whether it must provide expenditure schedule copies to customers as previously advised by FPPC in 1988, citing implementation burden and minimal public benefit.
